title: The Colonial Clippers
author: Lubbock, Basil, 1876-1944

quoteFirst: From this place nothing would induce the creature to move; and feeling astonished at its fidelity and affection, we let it lie during the day; though strange to say, it manifested a desire to leave the room at night, returning always
quoteLast: , and if the door was shut, mewing till it gained admittance. On the funeral-day, the faithful creature did not seem to understand the absence of its master; it left the room upon the removal of the body; but the first thing we saw when the mourners returned was the poor pussie lying at the door of the chamber.
title: Chambers's Journal of Popular Literature, Science, and Art, No. 742, March 16, 1878
author: Various; Chambers, Robert, 1802-1871 [Editor]; Chambers, William, 1800-1883 [Editor]

quoteFirst: They had discovered that the enemy had guns and meant fighting. Lest he should follow, they sent out from Ladysmith,
quoteLast: , half a battalion apiece of the Devonshire and Manchester Regiments by train, and the 42nd Field Battery, with a squadron of the 5th Dragoon Guards, by road. They arrived, and there fell on us the common lot of reconnaissances.
title: From Capetown to Ladysmith: An Unfinished Record of the South African War
author: Steevens, G. W. (George Warrington), 1869-1900; Blackburn, Vernon, 1866-1907 [Editor]

quoteTime: About nine o'clock in the morning
quoteLast: we drew rations, and soon had hot coffee, which put us all right again; but it was nearly noon before we could obtain grain for our horses, by which time they were very hungry, having had nothing to eat for about forty-two hours.
title: Battery D First Rhode Island Light Artillery in the Civil War
author: Sumner, George C.

quoteLast: our outposts near the right flank of the English were already in touch with the enemy, and rifle-fire was heard at intervals.

quoteFirst: _Now we’re back at the Frosts’ house, and it’s a week after the events narrated in Act I. It is
quoteTime: about nine o’clock in the morning
quoteLast: , and through the open windows the sun is shining in great, brave squares upon the carpet. The jars, the glasses, the phials of a certain memorable night have been removed, but there is an air about the house quite inconsistent with the happy day outside, an air of catastrophe, a profound gloom that seems to have settled even upon the “Library of Wit and Humor” in the dingy bookcase.
title: The Vegetable; or, From President to Postman
author: Fitzgerald, F. Scott (Francis Scott), 1896-1940

quoteFirst: However, since it would be much better to go with the help' of Adolphe, I immediately inquired my way to the rue Pigale, and started off for it. After many turnings and twistings, I reached my destination at
quoteLast: . Adolphe was not yet up; but his father was walking in the garden. I went up to him. He stopped, let me approach, held out his hand to me, and said--
title: My Memoirs, Vol. II, 1822 to 1825
author: Dumas, Alexandre, 1802-1870; Waller, E. M. (Emily Mary) [Translator]
